papers.title: Heterofunctional Graph Theoretic Framework for Holistic Resiliency Analysis of Power Systems
papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems

papers.title: Socioeconomic Vulnerability-Based Resilience Assessment Metric for Power Distribution Systems
papers.abstract: Power distribution systems face increasing threats from extreme weather events, with disproportionate impacts on socioeconomically vulnerable communities. Traditional resilience metrics often overlook these disparities by treating communities as homogeneous, limiting their usefulness in equitable planning and operation. This paper proposes a novel, community-centric framework for resilience quantification that integrates socioeconomic vulnerability into conventional reliability-based measures. Specifically, we introduce modified versions of utility-familiar indices, including event-based Customer Minutes of Interruption (E-CMI) and Customer Average Interruption Duration Index (E-CAIDI), extended into weighted forms using a Generalized Community Vulnerability Score (GCVS). The GCVS captures demographic and economic factors derived from publicly available census data and industry reports, enabling spatiotemporally sensitive and computationally tractable resilience assessment. We demonstrate the framework through two case studies: 1) real-world outage and weather data from southwest Kansas; and 2) optimal planning of distributed energy resources on the IEEE 37-node test system. Results highlight that weighted metrics provide a more holistic and equitable representation of resilience, prioritizing vulnerable communities in restoration and planning decisions. The proposed approach bridges the gap between resilience research and field application, offering utilities practical tools to enhance both technical performance and community equity.

papers.title: Equity-driven Planning of Distributed Solar PV using Optimal Transport
papers.venue: IEEE Power & Energy Society General Meeting
papers.abstract: Typically, distribution system planning processes do not explicitly incorporate energy equity considerations, such as identifying consumers most affected by energy costs and determining how investments in the distribution system can address existing energy burden imbalances. This paper proposes a novel optimal transport (OT)-based method to improve the energy burden distribution of consumers. The approach involves the strategic siting and sizing of solar PV in order to assist customers with high energy burden and improve the overall energy burden distribution of the community. The desired energy burden distribution is defined using the equal distribution equivalent (EDE) concept. The OT-based method is then used to estimate the distributed solar PV capacity to be installed at various locations and the tariffs to be adjusted, all while improving the energy burden distribution and providing valuable insights into distributed generation (DG) planning. The results on IEEE 37 bus test system demonstrate how DG planning, considering EDE and OT, can help reduce the energy burden of low-income consumers. Additionally, the approach also reveals optimal tariff adjustments needed to ensure revenue neutrality for distribution utilities.

papers.title: Game Theoretic Coordination of Microgrids with Equity Considerations
papers.venue: 2024 IEEE Kansas Power and Energy Conference (KPEC)
papers.abstract: Microgrids (MG) are an effective way to enhance power system resiliency, thanks to the integration of distributed energy resources that enable both grid-connected and islanded operations. However, effective energy management of MG is a key challenge that needs to be addressed. In this paper, a day-ahead energy management framework is proposed for coordinated and non-coordinated microgrids to achieve effective scheduling of available resources. In coordinated operations, energy trading among MGs is allowed while being connected to the distribution utility grid. A Nash bargaining game-theoretic formulation is designed for proactive trading among the coordinating MGs. The unique contribution of the work is the explicit consideration of energy equity in the energy management of MGs. Simulation results show a 26% reduction in the average energy burden of the communities served by the MG from coordinated to non-coordinated operations.

papers.title: Cyber-Security for Smart Grid Control: Vulnerability Assessment, Attack Detection, and Mitigation
papers.venue: Transactions on Computer Systems and Networks

papers.title: Quantifying the Performance of Cluster based Anomaly Detection in Smart Grid Control
papers.venue: IEEE Power & Energy Society General Meeting
papers.abstract: Cyber attacks are a significant concern for power systems due to the integration of automation and control. With increasing zero-day attacks, anomaly based attack detection mechanisms have gained importance. Though multiple anomaly detection techniques have been introduced in the literature, an important factor to be considered is their performance quantification. In this paper we propose a Cluster based Hypothesis Test (CHT) for anomaly detection in power systems and quantify its performance. An theoretical detection threshold is provided under which the expected false alarm period of the CHT can be guaranteed to be smaller than a desired value. The detection probability is also calculated for the CHT to better quantify the accuracy of the algorithm.

papers.title: A Transductive Graph Neural Network learning for Grid Resilience Analysis
papers.venue: IEEE International Conference on Smart Grid Communications
papers.abstract: Power grids are critical infrastructures that require robust resilience analysis to ensure reliable and uninterrupted electricity supply. Traditional simulation-based methods for grid resilience analysis suffer from computational complexity and limited ability to capture the full spectrum of potential disruptions. This paper presents a novel approach to enhance grid resilience by leveraging transductive graph neural network (GNN) learning to identify critical nodes and links. By leveraging the graph structure and system features, GNNs effectively learn resilience metrics and accurately identify critical nodes based on actual grid operational behavior. The efficacy of the proposed approach is demonstrated through case studies on node criticality scoring and critical node/line identification in cascading outage scenarios. The results highlight the advantages of learning-based methods over traditional simulation-based approaches and their potential to revolutionize grid resilience analysis. The contributions of this paper include a graph-based scalable approach for fast cascading analysis, an inductive formulation for training GNN models, and a transfer learning-based approach to scale the model to large-scale power systems.

papers.title: ComPSim: A Community based Smart Grid Testbed for Holistic Resilience Analysis
papers.venue: IEEE International Conference on Smart Grid Communications
papers.abstract: This paper presents a novel approach to designing a smart grid testbed, that advances the state of knowledge on power system resilience analysis by including the social aspects of the community in simulation. The proposed Community-based Power Simulation virtual testbed, termed ComPSim herein, integrates non-physical dimensions of the community into the smart grid infrastructure system. The ComPSim test bed can be applied to identify the social impacts of power system disruptions. The testbed takes into account diverse socioeconomic characteristics of the community, enabling targeted analysis and policy formulation. This research highlights the need for integrating community’s social and economic facet into future infrastructure planning and decision efforts.
